text: According to the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG, 2015), which of the following is identified as a key predisposing risk factor for developing an Obstetric Anal Sphincter Injury (OASI)?
options: Elective caesarean section
Prolonged second stage of labour
Multiparity with rapid second stage
Maternal age under 20 years
explanation: Predisposing clinical factors for OASI include a prolonged second stage of labour, instrumental births (forceps or ventouse), high birth weight (macrosomia), shoulder dystocia, and primiparity.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When comparing the routine HPV vaccination guidelines between the UK and the US (CDC), at what routine age is the HPV vaccine typically administered in the UK immunisation schedule?
options: 9–10 years
12–13 years
15–16 years
18–21 years
explanation: In the UK, the routine HPV vaccination is offered to boys and girls aged 12 to 13 years (School Year 8).
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A nurse is planning antenatal education for a primigravid client at 34 weeks of gestation. Which non-pharmacological strategy is recommended antenatally to help reduce the risk of perineal trauma during childbirth?
options: Daily pelvic floor muscle exercises only
Daily warm tub baths
Antenatal perineal massage
Avoiding all weight-bearing exercises
explanation: Antenatal perineal massage, typically initiated around 32 to 36 weeks of gestation, helps stretch and soften the perineal tissues, reducing the risk of episiotomy and perineal tears.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: During an assessment, a female client from an area with a high prevalence of Female Genital Mutilation (FGM) is identified as having FGM Type III. What specific surgical intervention may be required prior to or during childbirth to allow a safe vaginal delivery?
options: Episiotomy only
Elective Caesarean Section
Defibulation
Cervical cerclage
explanation: For women with FGM Type III (infibulation), defibulation (a surgical procedure to open the sealed vaginal introitus) is recommended antenatally or intrapartum to allow safe vaginal birth and prevent severe tissue damage.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: In the context of women's and reproductive health, how does the World Health Organization (WHO, 2009) define "Women's Health"?
options: Health issues that are exclusively related to pregnancy and childbearing
The branch of healthcare focusing only on hormonal changes during menopause
Health issues specific to women, or those that affect women differently or more commonly than men
The complete absence of gynecological disease throughout the lifespan
explanation: The WHO (2009) defines women's health holistically, extending beyond reproduction to include any issues specific to women or those presenting differently or more frequently in women than in men.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is considered a primary component of preventive gynecological care that nurses actively promote to reduce cervical cancer mortality?
options: Annual pelvic floor muscle training
Cervical screening (Smear test) and HPV vaccination
Screening for gestational diabetes
Routine pelvic ultrasound scans
explanation: Cervical screening and HPV vaccination are the two most effective public health interventions for the primary prevention and early detection of cervical cancers.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which of the following is a key physical promotion strategy recommended antenatally to support pelvic health and improve bladder control?
options: Strict bed rest starting from the second trimester
High-impact aerobic training
Pelvic floor (Kegel) exercises
Increasing carbonated beverage intake
explanation: Regular pelvic floor muscle exercises strengthen the supportive muscles of the bladder, uterus, and bowel, preventing incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the main clinical objective of the "OASI Care Bundle" used in UK maternity units?
options: To completely replace the need for active management of the third stage of labour
To standardize interventions during birth to reduce the incidence of severe perineal tears
To promote water births for all high-risk pregnancies
To avoid the use of instrumental births in all scenarios
explanation: The Obstetric Anal Sphincter Injury (OASI) Care Bundle is an evidence-based set of practices designed to minimize third- and fourth-degree perineal tears during vaginal births.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: At what gestational age range is it ideal to educate pregnant women regarding perineal massage and pelvic health preparation?
options: 12 to 16 weeks
20 to 24 weeks
32 to 36 weeks
Only once active labour commences
explanation: Education regarding pelvic floor health, perineal massage, and OASI prevention is ideally delivered between 32 and 36 weeks of gestation.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which social determinant is recognized as a major factor influencing a woman's access to reproductive health screenings?
options: Intracellular potassium levels
Socio-economic status and healthcare literacy
Genetic mutations in type III collagen
Rate of hepatic first-pass metabolism
explanation: Socio-economic status, education, geographical location, and healthcare literacy significantly shape a woman's ability to access and engage with screening services.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A nurse is discussing pelvic floor muscle exercises. What is the recommended frequency and duration for these exercises to be effective?
options: Once a week for 30 minutes
Multiple times daily, performing both slow and fast contractions consistently
Only when experiencing active urinary leakage
100 consecutive fast contractions without resting
explanation: Pelvic floor muscle training requires regular, daily sessions combining slow/endurance holds and fast/power contractions to build tone and coordination.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The incidence of pelvic floor dysfunction, such as pelvic organ prolapse, is often difficult to define epidemiologically because of:
options: A complete lack of diagnostic imaging tools
Shame, stigma, and underreporting by affected women
The condition resolving spontaneously in all cases
Its restriction to women under the age of 25
explanation: Shame and social stigma surrounding pelvic floor symptoms mean many women suffer in silence, leading to a high gap between estimated prevalence and diagnosed cases.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: In the context of maternal care, "shoulder dystocia" is classified as:
options: A normal physiological position during the first stage of labour
An obstetric emergency where the baby's shoulder becomes wedged behind the mother's pubic bone
A metabolic condition affecting neonatal calcium levels
A contraindication to receiving the HPV vaccine
explanation: Shoulder dystocia is a major obstetric emergency that increases the risk of maternal perineal trauma (OASI) and neonatal injury.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which dietary and lifestyle advice is recommended to optimize pelvic and bowel health in women?
options: Restricting dietary fiber to prevent bowel movements
Adequate fluid intake, high-fiber diet, and smoking cessation
High caffeine intake to stimulate bladder contraction
Avoiding physical activity or walking
explanation: A high-fiber diet and hydration prevent constipation and straining (which weakens the pelvic floor), while smoking cessation reduces chronic coughing, another risk factor for prolapse.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Why is a multi-disciplinary approach (including midwives, physiotherapists, and obstetricians) vital in women's pelvic health?
options: It eliminates the patient's need to take personal responsibility for care.
It provides holistic, integrated management from prenatal prevention to postnatal rehabilitation.
It restricts patients from seeking primary care reviews.
It converts all standard gynecological care into wholly compensatory systems.
explanation: Pelvic health involves a continuum of preventative education, active intrapartum protection, and specialized physical therapy for optimal recovery. Presentation 2: Seminar - Women's & Reproductive Health
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: How does the World Health Organization (WHO, 2006) define "Reproductive Health" holistically?
options: The complete absence of physical reproductive tract disease during pregnancy
A state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being in all matters relating to the reproductive system, functions, and processes
The capacity to reproduce successfully up to the age of 45
Achieving clinical perfection in prenatal diagnostic test results
explanation: The WHO definition of reproductive health is holistic, emphasizing complete physical, mental, and social well-being rather than just the absence of disease.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When applying a sensitive, trauma-informed approach to cervical screening (smear tests), which action is most appropriate for the nurse?
options: Performing the speculum examination quickly without explanation to save time
Explaining each step clearly, obtaining active consent, offering a chaperone, and allowing the client control over the pace of the procedure
Telling the client that pain is completely imaginary and must be ignored
Mandating that the client's family members remain in the exam room
explanation: A sensitive, trauma-informed approach is vital in cervical screening to support anxiety, protect dignity, and build trust.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: According to Ansari (2024), adopting a highly sensitive and individualized approach during cervical screening directly influences:
options: The biochemical sensitivity of the laboratory HPV assay
Uptake rates, patient trust, and overall screening coverage across diverse or vulnerable populations
The half-life of the speculum lubricant
The physiological thickness of the vaginal epithelial strata
explanation: Patient-centered and culturally sensitive communication reduces barriers to screening, directly boosting screening participation among underserved groups.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Under a life-course approach to women’s health, which key transitions must nurses recognize as having unique physical and psychological implications?
options: Only pregnancy and childbirth
Menarche, reproductive years, pregnancy, postpartum, and perimenopause/menopause
Hepatic first-pass clearance and renal filtration shifts
Standard triage streaming categories
explanation: A life-course framework recognizes that health outcomes are shaped by experiences across a woman's entire lifespan, from puberty to post-menopause.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Why is it clinically incorrect to assume that "reproductive health" only concerns pregnant women?
options: Non-pregnant women do not possess a reproductive system.
It excludes crucial dimensions like menstrual health, contraception, sexual well-being, fertility, and menopause.
Pregnancy is the only phase where reproductive organs are metabolically active.
Standard nursing models only apply during the prenatal period.
explanation: Holistic reproductive health encompasses all stages of life and physical/social aspects of the reproductive system, far beyond the scope of gestation alone.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The phrase "complete well-being" in the WHO definition of health is best understood as:
options: Achieving a flawless state of physical and mental performance
A broad, multidimensional view of health that includes psychological and social dimensions, rather than a lack of symptoms
An unrealistic standard that cannot be applied to modern nursing
The absolute rejection of all pharmacological interventions
explanation: Modern health theories (like Schramme, 2023) clarify that "complete" denotes breadth (addressing physical, mental, and social aspects of life) rather than requiring perfection.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When a nurse provides contraceptive counseling, which clinical principle is paramount to support patient autonomy?
options: Directing the client to the method the nurse personally prefers
Providing objective, evidence-based information on all options to facilitate shared decision-making
Choosing a method based solely on the cheapest institutional cost
Insisting that the client's partner makes the final decision
explanation: Respecting autonomy means offering comprehensive, unbiased contraceptive education so the individual can make an informed choice that suits their life.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which of the following is a recognized barrier to cervical screening uptake among young or marginalized women?
options: High level of digital literacy
Fear of pain, embarrassment, or previous traumatic experiences
Excessive availability of open-access screening clinics
Standardized HPV vaccination schedules
explanation: Barriers to screening include fear, embarrassment, pain, past trauma, cultural taboos, or lack of knowledge about the procedure's purpose.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A life-course approach to women's health highlights that adult cardiovascular and bone health can be heavily influenced by:
options: Childhood nutrition, physical activity, and adolescent lifestyle habits
The exact time of day a medication is administered
The choice of emergency triage color category in youth
Using manual rather than automated blood pressure cuffs
explanation: Health behaviors and nutritional states in early life phases build baseline reserves (e.g., peak bone mass), directly influencing risks (like osteoporosis) in later years.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the role of the nurse when a client discloses they have experienced gender-based violence?
options: To investigate the incident forensically without patient consent
To provide a safe, non-judgmental space, offer immediate clinical care, and signpost to specialized support services
To advise the patient to ignore the incident to avoid social stigma
To document it only if physical injuries are visible
explanation: Safeguarding and trauma-informed care require active listening, validation, addressing physical/mental needs, and connecting the survivor to support pathways.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Menopause is clinically defined as the point in time when a woman has not had a menstrual period for:
options: 3 consecutive months
6 consecutive months
12 consecutive months
24 consecutive months
explanation: Menopause is diagnosed retrospectively after 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea without other physiological or pathological causes.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: How can nurses advocate for "menstrual equity" in their clinical or community practice?
options: By limiting education regarding menstrual hygiene to adult populations only
Promoting access to free sanitary products, reducing period stigma, and educating on menstrual disorders
Requiring all female patients to use hormonal suppression therapies
Eliminating menstrual history taking from baseline assessments
explanation: Menstrual equity involves ensuring all individuals have access to the products, education, and supportive environments needed to manage menstruation safely and with dignity.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Why is it essential for nurses to screen for perinatal depression during antenatal and postnatal visits?
options: Mental health changes during this period are always self-limiting and require no intervention.
Perinatal mental health disorders are common and can significantly impact both maternal well-being and infant development.
Prescribing antidepressants is mandatory for all postpartum patients.
Standard care plans do not cover physical complications.
explanation: Early identification and management of perinatal mood disorders (such as depression or psychosis) are vital to protect maternal-infant safety and support bonding.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A client states they are hesitant to attend cervical screening because of their cultural background. How should the nurse respond?
options: "If you do not attend, you are being non-compliant with standard healthcare guidelines."
"I understand your concerns; let's discuss how we can adapt the environment or procedure to make you feel as comfortable and respected as possible."
"There is no alternative, so you must overcome your embarrassment."
"We can bypass screening if you have received the HPV vaccine."
explanation: Delivering culturally sensitive care involves validating the client's feelings and identifying collaborative adaptations to facilitate safe, comfortable care.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What does the term "holistic care" mean when applied to reproductive health nursing?
options: Focusing solely on the physiological functions of the pelvic organs
Integrating the physical, psychological, cultural, social, and spiritual dimensions of the patient into the care plan
Bypassing evidence-based treatments in favor of alternative therapies only
Standardizing care to treat all women as an identical cohort
explanation: Holistic care recognizes that a woman's reproductive well-being is deeply interconnected with her emotional, social, and cultural context, requiring individualized care planning. Presentation 3: Global Health and Inequalities
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which of the following best defines "Global Health" as an academic and clinical discipline?
options: The study of health issues that only affect low-income countries
An area for study, research, and practice that places a priority on improving health and achieving equity in health for all people worldwide
The administrative distribution of pharmaceutical medical patents
Establishing standardized triage color codes across European hospitals
explanation: Global health focuses on transnational health issues and determinants, aiming to improve health outcomes and reduce health disparities globally.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: During the period of 2018–2023, the World Health Organization tracked the progress of its Sustainable Development Goals. Which of the following was identified as a major remaining challenge for building "healthier populations"?
options: A global surplus of clean drinking water
Declining rates of global physical inactivity
Air quality, tobacco consumption, and obesity
Too many individuals achieving universal health coverage
explanation: Environmental degradation, rising obesity rates, sedentary lifestyles, and tobacco use remain major global barriers to population health.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is meant by the term "Universal Health Coverage" (UHC) under the Sustainable Development Goals?
options: Ensuring all citizens globally are prescribed the same medications
That all people have access to needed health services of high quality without experiencing financial hardship
Restricting healthcare access to those with private medical insurance
Shifting all health systems to wholly compensatory models of care
explanation: The core of UHC is ensuring equitable access to care, treatment, and prevention without pushing families into poverty or debt.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: In global public health, what is a "health inequality" (or health disparity)?
options: The biological variations in height and eye color among populations
Systematic, avoidable, and unjust differences in health status, survival, and access to healthcare between different population groups
The difference in active drug bioavailability between oral and IV routes
A normal physiological adaptation to environmental changes
explanation: Health inequalities are not random biological differences; they are systematic variations driven by social, economic, and political factors.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: In 2025, the UK House of Commons announced plans to adjust its Official Development Assistance (ODA) budget. What was the projected target for UK aid funding starting from 2027?
options: Increasing aid to 1.5% of Gross National Income (GNI)
Reducing aid to 0.3% of Gross National Income (GNI)
Setting aid at a flat rate of $\$1$ billion annually
Eliminating all international development spending entirely
explanation: The UK House of Commons outlined a reduction in aid spending to 0.3% of GNI from 2027, highlighting policy shifts that affect global health funding.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: According to epidemiological estimates surrounding the COVID-19 pandemic, how did the actual global excess mortality compare to the official recorded COVID-19 deaths?
options: The actual excess mortality was identical to the official count.
The actual excess mortality was estimated to be up to three times higher than the official recorded figures.
The actual excess mortality was significantly lower than the official records.
No reliable data exists to measure excess mortality.
explanation: WHO estimates suggest actual excess mortality during the pandemic was roughly three times higher than officially registered COVID-19 fatalities due to underreporting and disrupted healthcare systems.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The term "Social Determinants of Health" (SDOH) refers to:
options: The genetic profile and chromosomal sequence of an individual
The conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work, and age, which shape their health outcomes
The chemical binding affinity of a drug receptor
The layout of an intensive care resuscitation bay
explanation: SDOH are non-medical factors (like housing, income, education, and employment) that have a massive influence on systemic health inequalities.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which global indicator measures the average number of years a newborn is expected to live in "full health," free from chronic disease or disability?
options: Healthy Life Expectancy (HALE)
Crude Birth Rate (CBR)
Infant Mortality Rate (IMR)
Gross National Income (GNI)
explanation: Unlike overall life expectancy, HALE adjusts for years lived with disability or illness, capturing the quality of life years in a population.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is a "double burden of disease" commonly observed in rapidly developing low- and middle-income countries?
options: Managing both physical pain and high pharmaceutical costs
Facing high rates of both infectious diseases (malaria, TB) and chronic non-communicable diseases (diabetes, cardiovascular disease)
Having to coordinate both primary and secondary nursing models
Addressing both water-soluble and fat-soluble drug side effects
explanation: Many developing nations face a double burden as they combat ongoing infectious diseases while seeing a rise in lifestyle-related chronic conditions.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When planning a global health intervention, why is it vital to assess "acceptability" within the target community?
options: To satisfy international trademark guidelines for medical software
To ensure the intervention aligns with local cultural values, beliefs, and practices, supporting trust and sustained participation
To bypass the need for local governmental approval
To standardize treatment costs across all participating nations
explanation: Interventions that ignore local culture, taboos, or social structures often face resistance, leading to project failure despite being technically sound.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The concept of "brain drain" in global health refers to:
options: The progressive cognitive decline observed in advanced stages of dementia
The migration of highly trained healthcare professionals from low-resource countries to wealthier nations, worsening local staff shortages
The loss of memory caused by prolonged stays in intensive care units
Algorithmic bias within clinical decision support systems
explanation: Brain drain strips developing health systems of their trained human resources, widening the global gap in healthcare capacity and quality.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which of the following serves as a key structural determinant of health inequalities within a city or region?
options: Access to reliable public transport and clean green spaces
The specific brand of stethoscope used by hospital staff
The chemical half-life of oral insulin
The color of the hospital's triage registration desks
explanation: Environmental design, transportation, housing, and access to clean spaces directly shape physical activity, stress levels, and exposure to pollution.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the primary role of Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) in global health delivery?
options: To write national health legislation and enforce taxes
To provide targeted aid, healthcare services, and advocacy, particularly in crisis zones or underserved areas
To manufacture and distribute all pharmaceutical medications globally
To replace the World Health Organization's legal authority
explanation: NGOs fill gaps in state infrastructure, delivering humanitarian assistance, training local staff, and piloting health innovations.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: How does a country's Gross National Income (GNI) per capita correlate with its average life expectancy?
options: There is no statistical relationship between income and survival.
Countries with higher GNI per capita generally have higher life expectancies due to better infrastructure, nutrition, and healthcare access.
Low GNI per capita is associated with a complete absence of chronic disease.
High GNI per capita automatically eliminates the risk of infectious diseases.
explanation: Economic prosperity supports the public infrastructure, sanitation, nutrition, and health services needed to sustain long-term population health.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When implementing global health solutions, what does "feasibility" refer to?
options: The maximum cost-effectiveness of a drug's hepatic metabolism
The practical capability of an intervention to be successfully executed given available resources, staff, and local infrastructure
The speed at which an AI diagnostic tool generates a recommendation
The transition of a patient from intensive care to a standard ward
explanation: Feasibility assesses whether a plan can realistically work in the real-world conditions of the target setting, avoiding overly complex strategies that local staff cannot maintain. Presentation 4: Integumentary System & Wound Healing
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The skin is divided into distinct layers. Which of the following represents the correct sequence of the epidermal strata, starting from the deepest germinal layer to the outermost surface?
options: Corneum, Granulosum, Spinosum, Basal
Basal, Spinosum, Granulosum, Corneum
Basal, Granulosum, Spinosum, Corneum
Corneum, Spinosum, Granulosum, Basal
explanation: The epidermis grows from the deepest germinal layer (basal) through the spiny layer (spinosum), the flattening keratogenous layer (granulosum), to the dead, keratin-packed outer layer (corneum).
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: If a severe burn or trauma completely destroys the basal layer of the epidermis, what is the clinical consequence regarding skin regeneration?
options: The skin will regenerate rapidly from the hair follicles.
The skin will not be able to self-regenerate and may require an autograft.
The basement membrane will immediately manufacture new basal cells.
The skin will heal exclusively by primary intention without scarring.
explanation: The basal layer contains the germinal stem cells required for epidermal renewal. Its destruction halts natural re-epithelialization, requiring surgical grafts.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which specialized cells located within the epidermis are responsible for providing protection against harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation by producing melanin?
options: Langerhans cells
Merkel cells
Melanocytes
Keratinocytes
explanation: Melanocytes synthesize the pigment melanin, which absorbs UV light to protect cell nuclei from radiation damage.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A nurse is managing a surgical incision where the wound edges are clean, closely approximated with sutures, and have minimal tissue loss. This wound is healing by which mechanism?
options: Secondary intention
Primary intention
Delayed primary closure
Granulation tissue formation
explanation: Primary intention occurs when clean wound edges are brought adjacent to each other, minimizing the space for granulation and reducing scar formation.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A patient has a large pressure ulcer that is left open to heal. The wound cavity is slowly filling with red, moist, vascular tissue composed of new capillaries and collagen. What is this tissue called?
options: Keloid scar tissue
Granulation tissue
Eschar tissue
Epidermal strata
explanation: Granulation tissue is the vascularized, fragile connective tissue that forms during the proliferative phase of secondary intention wound healing.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the primary cellular event that characterizes the "remodelling" phase of wound healing?
options: Migration of Langerhans cells to trigger inflammation
Conversion of temporary Type III collagen to stronger, organized Type I collagen
Rapid mitosis of basal cells to close the wound margin
Massive leakage of plasma proteins from local capillaries
explanation: Remodeling (maturation) involves restructuring collagen fibers to increase tensile strength, transitioning from flexible Type III to scar-associated Type I collagen.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which specialized epidermal cells function as macrophages, playing a key role in the skin's immune defense by presenting antigens to T-lymphocytes?
options: Merkel cells
Melanocytes
Langerhans cells
Keratinocytes
explanation: Langerhans cells are dendritic immunological cells in the epidermis that identify, capture, and present foreign antigens to initiate an immune response.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A nurse is assessing a complex wound that was purposely left open for 5 days to clear an infection before being surgically closed with grafts. This method of healing is called:
options: Primary intention
Secondary intention
Delayed primary closure (or tertiary intention)
Natural granulation
explanation: Delayed primary closure is used for contaminated wounds, allowing drainage and observation before surgical closure is completed.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the complex, specialized zone situated between the epidermis and the dermis that anchors the two layers together?
options: Stratum corneum
Basement membrane zone (BMZ)
Subcutaneous adipose tissue
Reticular dermis
explanation: The BMZ is a structural interface composed of collagen and laminin that anchors the epidermis to the underlying dermis, facilitating nutrient transport.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which cells are responsible for light touch sensory perception in the basal layer of the skin?
options: Melanocytes
Merkel cells
Langerhans cells
Fibroblasts
explanation: Merkel cells are specialized neuroendocrine cells in the basal epidermis that pair with sensory nerve endings to detect gentle tactile stimuli.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The dermis is primarily composed of which structural proteins, providing elasticity and tensile strength to the skin?
options: Keratin and Melanin
Collagen and Elastin
Actin and Myosin
Hemoglobin and Myoglobin
explanation: Fibroblasts in the dermis produce a dense matrix of collagen (for strength) and elastin (for flexibility and stretch).
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A nurse notes yellow, devitalized slough in the center of a patient's leg ulcer. What is the nurse's priority action regarding this tissue?
options: Leaving it intact to protect the wound bed
Encouraging debridement to remove dead tissue and promote granulation
Massaging the area vigorously to increase local perfusion
Applying a dry gauze dressing without any cleaning
explanation: Slough consists of dead cells and debris that blocks granulation; removing it via debridement is essential to progress the healing cycle.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Why do surgical wounds closed by primary intention heal faster and with less scarring than those healing by secondary intention?
options: Primary intention wounds bypass the inflammatory phase entirely.
The approximated edges minimize the volume of granulation tissue and epithelial migration required to seal the wound.
Primary intention does not involve collagen synthesis.
They are kept completely dry without any cellular activity.
explanation: Bringing the edges together minimizes tissue loss, requiring less cellular energy, granulation, and remodeling, which results in a smaller scar.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: During which phase of wound healing do local capillaries dilate, allowing white blood cells and plasma to enter the tissue, causing swelling and redness?
options: Hemostasis phase
Inflammatory phase
Proliferative phase
Maturation phase
explanation: The inflammatory phase is characterized by vasodilation and increased vascular permeability to deliver neutrophils and macrophages to clean the wound.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which systemic factor is most critical to support collagen synthesis and cellular repair in a patient with a large healing wound?
options: High intake of simple carbohydrates
Adequate protein, Vitamin C, and zinc intake
Restricting fluid intake to minimize wound drainage
High doses of anti-inflammatory steroids
explanation: Protein provides the amino acids needed for collagen synthesis, while Vitamin C and zinc are vital cofactors for cellular repair and tissue building. Presentation 5: Introduction to Critical Care Nursing
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: According to Kayambankadzanja (2022), "Critical Care" is defined as a state of ill health characterized by:
options: Stable chronic disease that requires outpatient management
Vital organ dysfunction, a high risk of imminent death if care is not provided, and the potential for reversibility
Any injury that requires immediate emergency department triage
The complete loss of cognitive function in late-stage dementia
explanation: The definition of critical care emphasizes the severity of organ dysfunction, the high risk of mortality, and the key criterion of reversibility.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: According to the Department of Health (2014) guidelines, "Critical Care" is defined not by a discrete clinical unit, but rather by:
options: The presence of mechanical ventilators
The level of care needed by the critically ill patient, wherever they are located
The ratio of physicians to nursing staff on a ward
The daily financial budget allocated to the intensive care unit
explanation: Critical care is a service concept defined by patient needs and acuity, meaning critical care principles must be applied wherever the deteriorating patient is.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the primary clinical difference between High Dependency Care and Intensive Care as classified by the British Association of Critical Care Nurses (BACCN, 2024)?
options: High Dependency Care requires a 1:1 nursing ratio, while Intensive Care is 1:2.
High Dependency Care involves patients requiring more intensive observation and risk assessment; Intensive Care involves patients with multi-organ failure requiring life support.
High Dependency Care is exclusively for post-operative orthopedic patients.
Intensive Care is defined solely by the patient’s age.
explanation: High dependency care focuses on close observation and preventing deterioration, while intensive care manages active organ failure requiring mechanical ventilation, vasoactive drugs, or renal replacement therapy.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When assessing a critically ill patient who is at risk of clinical deterioration, which structured tool is recommended by NICE (2017) to track vital signs and alert for early intervention?
options: The Abbey Pain Scale
The National Early Warning Score (NEWS/NEWS2)
The Roper-Logan-Tierney dependence card
The SBAR escalation sheet
explanation: NEWS2 is a standardized scoring system that tracks physiological parameters to detect early signs of clinical deterioration, prompting timely escalation.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: During a systematic ABCDE assessment of a deteriorating patient, which airway sign indicates a partial airway obstruction requiring immediate intervention?
options: Bilateral lung crackles on auscultation
Snoring, gurgling, or stridor
A respiratory rate of 16 breaths per minute
Capillary refill time of 2 seconds
explanation: Adventitious airway sounds (stridor, snoring, wheezing, gurgling) are key signs of partial airway compromise that must be managed in the "A" phase of the assessment.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When evaluating the "B" (Breathing) parameter of the ABCDE framework, what objective assessment findings should the critical care nurse prioritize?
options: Capillary refill time and peripheral pulse rate
Respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, chest expansion symmetry, and breath sounds
Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) and pupillary light reflex
Blood glucose levels and core body temperature
explanation: Evaluating breathing involves checking respiratory rate and effort, chest movement symmetry, oxygen saturations, and auscultating for air entry or abnormal sounds.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A patient in the ICU has a central venous catheter (CVC) in place. Why is maintaining strict aseptic technique during CVC care critical?
options: To speed up the rate of drug excretion by the kidneys
To prevent Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infections (CLABSIs)
To stabilize the patient's hypercapnic respiratory drive
To avoid Type B idiosyncratic drug reactions
explanation: CVCs provide direct access to the central venous circulation, making strict asepsis during line access and dressing changes vital to prevent systemic infections.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Excessive noise in the Intensive Care Unit has been researched extensively. According to Darbyshire (2016), high noise levels are:
options: Beneficial because they keep patients alert and oriented
Bad for staff performance and very bad for patient sleep, recovery, and delirium risk
Essential to ensure all alarms are heard clearly across the ward
Completely unavoidable and carry no physiological consequences
explanation: Noise pollution in the ICU disrupts sleep, increases stress hormones, delays healing, and is a major risk factor for ICU psychosis and delirium.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which sleep hygiene strategy has been shown to reduce the incidence of delirium in intensive care units, as supported by Litton et al. (2016)?
options: Leaving all overhead ward lights on continuously
The use of earplugs and eye masks to support natural sleep patterns
Administering high-dose sedatives to all patients at night
Restricting family visits to nighttime hours only
explanation: Promoting natural circadian rhythms through non-pharmacological sleep hygiene interventions (like earplugs, eye masks, and quiet hours) lowers delirium risk.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: In critical care communication, what is the core clinical focus of the "ReSPECT" process (Recommended Summary of Plan for Emergency Care and Treatment)?
options: Standardizing the financial billing for ICU therapies
Creating a personalized plan for a person's clinical care in a future emergency when they may lack capacity
Mandating resuscitation for all admitted patients regardless of prognosis
Documenting the patient's historical volume of distribution
explanation: The ReSPECT process is a collaborative planning tool used to establish emergency care preferences, including CPR decisions, before a crisis occurs.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Under the "D" (Disability) phase of the ABCDE framework, what is the primary neurological assessment tool used to evaluate a patient's level of consciousness?
options: The Abbey Pain Scale
The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) or AVPU scale
The Manchester Triage checklist
The RLT Activity of Living map
explanation: Assessing neurological status involves checking conscious level (via GCS or AVPU), pupillary size and reaction, and blood glucose.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When assessing a deteriorating patient's circulation ("C"), which clinical sign suggests early hypovolemic compensation?
options: Warm, flushed skin and bradycardia
Cool, pale, clammy skin, tachycardia, and prolonged capillary refill time
Sudden hyperventilation and respiratory alkalosis
Intracellular fluid shifts into the transcellular space
explanation: The body compensates for fluid loss or low cardiac output by vasoconstricting peripheral vessels and increasing heart rate to maintain perfusion to vital organs.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Self-care and resilience strategies for critical care nurses are essential to prevent:
options: Type A exaggerated drug reactions
Compassion fatigue, burnout, and moral distress
Fluctuations in patient early warning scores
Administrative record auditing errors
explanation: The high-stress, high-acuity environment of critical care puts nursing staff at high risk for psychological burnout, requiring robust institutional and personal self-care strategies.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Why is "potential for reversibility" a key criterion when admitting a patient to an Intensive Care Unit?
options: Intensive care beds are designed to be used only for short-term stays.
Invasive, high-acuity life support interventions are appropriate only when there is a realistic prospect of recovery or quality of life.
Reversibility is a legal requirement under the UK Data Protection Act.
Standard nursing models do not work on irreversible conditions.
explanation: ICU care is highly invasive and associated with major complications; clinical utility dictates that these therapies are targeted where they can restore functioning or bridge a reversible crisis. Presentation 6: Principles and Aims of Intravenous Fluid Therapy
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: In an adult body, what percentage of lean body weight is typically accounted for by water?
explanation: Water is the largest single component of the human body, accounting for roughly 55% to 60% of total body mass in lean adults.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The total body water is divided into discrete compartments. What percentage of total body weight is represented by Intracellular Fluid (ICF)?
explanation: Intracellular fluid (the cytosol of cells) constitutes the largest fluid compartment, representing roughly 42% of total body weight.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The Extracellular Fluid (ECF) is divided into several sub-compartments. Which of the following is the major component of ECF that surrounds cells but does not actively circulate?
options: Plasma (Intravascular fluid)
Interstitial Fluid (ISF)
Transcellular Fluid (TCF)
Cytosol
explanation: Interstitial fluid makes up the bulk of the ECF, filling the gaps and spaces between cells.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Fluid compartments are separated by barriers. What type of physical barrier primarily separates the intracellular fluid (ICF) from the surrounding interstitial fluid (ISF)?
options: The capillary basement membrane
The semi-permeable cell membrane
The blood-brain barrier
The alveolar-capillary membrane
explanation: The cell membrane serves as the barrier between the inside and outside of the cell, allowing the passage of water and selective movement of solutes.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A nurse is preparing to administer an intravenous fluid that has an osmolarity similar to that of normal blood plasma. This type of fluid is classified as:
options: Hypertonic crystalloid
Isotonic crystalloid
Hypotonic crystalloid
Hyperoncotic colloid
explanation: Isotonic fluids (such as 0.9% Sodium Chloride) have an osmolarity close to blood plasma, causing minimal fluid shifts across semi-permeable membranes.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the primary clinical danger associated with the rapid administration of a hypertonic intravenous fluid?
options: Cellular swelling and rupture (lysis)
Cellular dehydration and shrinkage due to water shifting out of the cells
Immediate loss of renal excretion capability
An increase in the drug's first-pass hepatic metabolism
explanation: Hypertonic solutions draw water out of the intracellular space into the vascular compartment, which can cause cellular dehydration.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Colloids differ from crystalloids because colloids contain:
options: Small, completely dissolved mineral salts that pass freely through membranes
Large, insoluble molecules (such as proteins or starches) that remain in the vascular space and exert oncotic pressure
Only water and glucose molecules
Lipid-soluble compounds designed to cross the blood-brain barrier
explanation: Colloids do not readily cross the capillary membrane, keeping fluid within the intravascular space to expand blood volume.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A patient is administered 5% Dextrose in water ($D_5W$). What happens physiologically to this solution once it enters the patient's systemic circulation?
options: It remains entirely in the intravascular space as a colloid.
The glucose is rapidly metabolized by cells, leaving behind free water that distributes evenly across all fluid compartments.
It causes immediate red blood cell lysis due to persistent hypertonicity.
It is excreted unchanged by the kidneys within 5 minutes.
explanation: While isotonic in the bag, the metabolism of dextrose turns $D_5W$ into a hypotonic solution of free water, which distributes throughout both ICF and ECF.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is a key nursing responsibility when managing a patient receiving continuous intravenous fluid therapy?
options: Ensuring the fluid bag is kept completely flat on the patient's bed
Monitoring intake and output, tracking daily weights, and assessing for signs of fluid overload
Changing the administration tubing every 2 hours
Adjusting the infusion rate based on patient preferences alone
explanation: Nurses are responsible for monitoring fluid status (including checking for lung crackles and edema) to detect and prevent fluid overload.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which of the following is an objective clinical indicator that a patient's intravenous fluid resuscitation has been successful?
options: Development of a bounding pulse and bilateral lung crackles
Stabilization of blood pressure and heart rate, with urine output of $>0.5 \text{ mL/kg/hour}$
A sudden drop in oxygen saturations to under 90%
An immediate increase in body weight of 5 kg
explanation: Adequate perfusion during fluid resuscitation is indicated by stable hemodynamics, clear lungs, and adequate renal perfusion (measured by urine output).
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Speed shock is a systemic complication of IV therapy that occurs when:
options: An IV fluid is administered too slowly over several days.
A medication or fluid is introduced into the circulation too rapidly, causing toxic systemic concentrations.
The patient’s metabolic rate drops suddenly.
The cannula is accidentally displaced into the subcutaneous tissue.
explanation: Rapid infusion can overwhelm the cardiovascular system, causing sudden hypotension, syncope, chest tightness, and cardiac arrest.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the physiological role of the hormone Aldosterone in maintaining human fluid balance?
options: It stimulates the kidneys to excrete water while retaining potassium.
It promotes renal sodium and water reabsorption, expanding ECF volume.
It triggers immediate vasodilation in the pulmonary vessels.
It decreases systemic vascular resistance during shock.
explanation: Released in response to the renin-angiotensin system, aldosterone acts on the distal tubules to retain sodium and water, helping maintain blood volume and pressure.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A patient is found to have an IV site that is cool, pale, swollen, and painful to touch. The infusion has stopped running. The nurse should suspect:
options: Phlebitis
Infiltration or extravasation
Systemic infection
Speed shock
explanation: Infiltration is the leakage of non-vesicant IV fluid into the surrounding subcutaneous tissue, presenting with localized coolness, swelling, and discomfort.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which crystalloid solution is most appropriate for initial fluid resuscitation in a patient presenting with hypovolemic shock?
options: 5% Dextrose in water ($D_5W$)
0.45% Sodium Chloride (Half Normal Saline)
Balanced crystalloids (such as Hartmann’s Solution or Lactated Ringer’s)
7.2% Hypertonic Saline
explanation: Balanced crystalloids mimic the electrolyte composition of plasma, making them preferred for large-volume resuscitation to avoid hyperchloremic acidosis.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When caring for a patient on IV fluids, why is it essential to pay close attention to co-morbidities like congestive heart failure or renal failure?
options: These patients have a high rate of drug biotransformation.
These patients have a significantly reduced capacity to handle fluid shifts, putting them at high risk for rapid pulmonary edema.
These conditions increase the risk of Type B idiosyncratic drug reactions.
These patients require the exclusive use of colloid solutions.
explanation: Compromised cardiac or renal function impairs the body's ability to excrete or pump fluid, requiring cautious, low-volume, and closely monitored IV therapy. Presentation 7: Medicines Management
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the clinical difference between the generic name and the trade/brand name of a medication?
options: The generic name is the official chemical name of the active compound; the trade name is the commercial brand name given by the manufacturer.
The generic name can have multiple variations, while there is only ever one trade name.
The trade name is legally approved for pediatric use only.
The generic name represents the inactive binders in the tablet.
explanation: A drug has one unique generic name reflecting its active ingredient, but can be sold under various trade names by different pharmaceutical companies.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the primary role of the British National Formulary (BNF) in UK clinical practice?
options: To track the hourly financial expenditure of hospital wards
To provide a comprehensive, authoritative reference guide on prescribing, pharmacology, and drug administration safety
To mandate standard emergency triage protocols
To record the legal power of attorney records for admitted patients
explanation: The BNF is the standard, up-to-date reference used by UK clinicians to verify drug dosages, indications, side effects, and interactions.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A nurse is preparing to administer a medication and notes the abbreviation "PRN" on the prescription sheet. What does this mean?
options: Administer the medication strictly at bedtime.
The medication should be given as required or when necessary.
The dose must be split between oral and IV routes.
The medication is restricted to patients with a narrow therapeutic index.
explanation: PRN stands for pro re nata , indicating the drug is given as needed within specified dosing intervals.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When reviewing a medication order, the nurse notes the abbreviation "QDS." What is the correct dosing frequency for this instruction?
options: Once daily
Twice daily
Three times daily
Four times daily
explanation: QDS stands for quater die sumendus , instructing the medication to be taken four times a day.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Why must a nurse use the BNF to check for "drug-drug interactions" before administering a new prescription?
options: To ensure the patient is not charged twice for the medications.
To identify if combining the drugs will alter their efficacy, clearance, or cause dangerous side effects.
To verify if the drugs are compatible with the RLT Activities of Living model.
To automate the patient's self-care agency scoring.
explanation: Co-administering drugs can lead to interactions that enhance toxicity or reduce therapeutic effect by competing for the same receptors or metabolic pathways.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the meaning of the abbreviation "PO" on a medication administration record?
options: Administer via the parenteral route only
Administer orally (by mouth)
Post-operative administration
Per rectum
explanation: PO stands for per os , indicating the oral route of administration.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When a nurse maintains a personal professional journal tracking the medications they commonly encounter in their practice area, which parameter is key to support safe care?
options: The retail pricing of the medications
The drug classification, indications, dosages, side effects, and specific nursing considerations
The corporate history of the manufacturing company
The color of the packaging box
explanation: Tracking clinical pharmacology parameters builds practical knowledge, helping the nurse anticipate side effects and deliver safer care.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A patient is prescribed a medication marked "M/R" (Modified Release). What is a critical safety consideration when administering this tablet?
options: It must be dissolved in hot water before swallowing.
It must not be crushed, chewed, or split, as this can cause rapid, toxic absorption of the entire dose.
It must be administered exclusively via the intravenous route.
It must be given on an empty stomach in all scenarios.
explanation: MR formulations are designed to release the drug slowly over time. Crushing destroys the release mechanism, causing a rapid, potentially toxic release of the active drug.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Under the NMC standards, if a nurse makes or identifies a medication error, what is their immediate professional duty?
options: Correcting the chart silently to avoid alarm
Being open and honest, reporting the error immediately to protect patient safety, and documenting the event accurately
Resigning from their clinical post immediately
Waiting for the weekly ward review to mention the incident
explanation: The duty of candor requires transparency when things go wrong, ensuring immediate corrective action is taken to protect the patient.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The abbreviation "BD" on a drug prescription instructions means:
options: Once daily
Twice daily
Three times daily
Every other day
explanation: BD stands for bis in die , instructing the dose to be administered twice a day.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What does the abbreviation "TDS" mean on a medical prescription?
options: Three times daily
Two times daily
To be discontinued immediately
Total daily dose
explanation: TDS stands for ter die sumendus , instructing the dose to be administered three times a day.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When a drug is marked as "Contraindicated" in a patient with liver failure, it means:
options: The drug should be administered with an enzyme blocker.
The drug must not be given to this patient because the risk of harm outweighs any potential benefit.
The drug can be given but only at double the standard dose.
The drug must be administered via the transdermal route only.
explanation: A contraindication is a specific situation in which a drug, procedure, or surgery should not be used because it may be harmful to the patient.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the purpose of the "double checking" process for high-risk medications (such as insulin or opioids)?
options: To increase the time spent on ward administration.
To provide an independent verification of the drug, dose, route, patient, and timing, reducing the risk of a catastrophic error.
To allow two nurses to share the legal liability of a mistake.
To comply with national tax guidelines.
explanation: High-alert medications have a narrow safety margin; a second independent check helps identify errors in calculation or selection before the drug reaches the patient.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which of the following is one of the "Rights" of medication administration that nurses must verify at the bedside?
options: Right Patient, Right Drug, Right Dose, Right Route, Right Time
Right Cost, Right Packaging, Right Ward, Right Doctor, Right Bed
Right Diagnosis, Right Prognosis, Right Diet, Right Exercise, Right Sleep
Right Clinic, Right Insurance, Right GNI, Right Triage, Right Model
explanation: The "five rights" (often expanded to seven or nine) are a fundamental safety framework to prevent administration errors.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A patient complains of stomach upset after taking a newly prescribed non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). Where in the BNF should the nurse look to see if this is a common side effect?
options: Under the "Contraindications" section
Under the "Adverse Effects" or "Side Effects" section
Under the "Medicinal Forms" pricing table
Under the "Dose and Administration" subsection
explanation: Expected or common side effects associated with a drug class are listed in the side effects section of the monograph. Presentation 8: Neurological Disorders
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: In the central nervous system, synaptic transmission involves the release of neurotransmitters. Which of the following is identified as the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter in the brain?
options: Acetylcholine (ACh)
Dopamine (DA)
Gamma-Aminobutyric Acid (GABA)
Norepinephrine (NA)
explanation: GABA is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter in the CNS, acting to hyperpolarize neurons and reduce action potential firing.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which of the following describes the cellular process of "excitotoxicity" leading to neuronal death?
options: Adequate oxygen perfusion causing cellular energy storage
Excessive activation of glutamate receptors leading to a massive influx of calcium ions, which activates destructive enzymes
The accumulation of extracellular amyloid plaques without cell damage
The normal physiological breakdown of GABA in the synaptic cleft
explanation: Glutamate excess over-activates NMDA receptors, allowing calcium to flood the cell, which triggers enzymatic self-digestion and cell death.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Neurodegenerative diseases often involve the process of "protein aggregation." What is the first step in this pathological cascade?
options: Apoptotic membrane blebbing
Misfolding of specific cellular proteins, causing them to lose function and clump together
Rapid regeneration of the basal epidermal layer
Hyper-filtration of cerebral spinal fluid
explanation: Protein aggregation begins with protein misfolding, exposing hydrophobic areas that cause the proteins to clump into toxic deposits.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the core pharmacological objective of drug therapy in Parkinson's disease?
options: To block all dopamine receptors in the motor cortex
To restore the balance of neurotransmitter levels, primarily by increasing dopamine activity or reducing acetylcholine activity
To increase the rate of GABA breakdown in the brainstem
To block the transport of calcium into the mitochondria
explanation: Parkinson's involves the loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra. Therapy aims to restore the balance between dopamine (inhibitory in this pathway) and acetylcholine (excitatory) to manage motor symptoms.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: How does a partial seizure differ from a generalized seizure?
options: Partial seizures always involve a complete loss of consciousness.
Partial seizures begin in one localized focus of the brain; generalized seizures involve both cerebral hemispheres from the onset.
Partial seizures are restricted to infants, while generalized seizures affect adults.
Partial seizures do not involve any alteration in ion channels.
explanation: Seizure classification is based on the site of onset: partial (focal) seizures start in a specific area, whereas generalized seizures involve widespread networks across both hemispheres.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: During an ischemic stroke (cerebrovascular accident), what is the primary challenge associated with re-establishing perfusion (thrombolysis)?
options: It increases the half-life of oral anticoagulants.
The risk of reperfusion injury and hemorrhagic transformation if blood flow is restored too late to damaged vessels.
It slows down the patient's respiratory rate.
It converts the ischemic area into a wholly compensatory system.
explanation: While restoring blood flow is vital, late reperfusion can introduce oxygen free radicals that damage fragile vessels, leading to bleeding into the brain.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Why are infants more susceptible to seizures affecting localized, small areas of the body compared to adults?
options: Their kidneys excrete GABA too rapidly.
Their intracortical connections are less developed, and their neurons are less capable of sustained, prolonged firing.
They have a higher density of Merkel cells.
They lack a functioning basement membrane.
explanation: Developing brains have incomplete myelination and synaptic pathways, which limits the spread of abnormal electrical discharges compared to adult brains.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Apoptosis differs from necrosis because apoptosis is:
options: A rapid, uncontrolled cell death caused by external trauma, resulting in severe inflammation
A programmed, highly regulated cell death that does not trigger a local inflammatory response
The process of cellular swelling and rupture
Restricted to muscle cells during exercise
explanation: Apoptosis is "cellular suicide" where the cell is systematically dismantled and cleared by macrophages without releasing inflammatory contents, unlike the messy rupture of necrosis.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Why is the use of anti-inflammatory steroids kept to a minimum in the long-term management of autoimmune neurological diseases?
options: They cause an immediate decrease in the volume of distribution.
They are associated with severe long-term side effects, including osteoporosis, immune suppression, diabetes, and adrenal suppression.
They completely block the action of GABA receptors.
They are ineffective at reducing neuro-inflammation.
explanation: While steroids are effective for acute exacerbations, their long-term systemic side effects require clinicians to minimize their use.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What are the two types of transmission that coordinate activity in the human nervous system?
options: Biological and Sociocultural
Electrical (along axons) and Chemical (at synapses)
Isotonic and Hypertonic
Primary and Secondary
explanation: Signal transmission involves electrical action potentials traveling down the nerve axon, followed by the release of chemical neurotransmitters across the synaptic cleft.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When an electrical impulse arrives at the pre-synaptic end plate, what ion is primarily responsible for triggering the exocytosis of neurotransmitter vesicles?
options: Sodium ($Na^+$)
Potassium ($K^+$)
Calcium ($Ca^{2+}$)
Chloride ($Cl^-$)
explanation: Action potential depolarization opens voltage-gated calcium channels, and the resulting calcium influx triggers vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter release.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A drug that blocks the reuptake of a neurotransmitter from the synaptic cleft will have what effect on synaptic transmission?
options: It will decrease the amount of neurotransmitter available to bind to receptors.
It will prolong and enhance the action of the neurotransmitter.
It will cause immediate apoptosis of the post-synaptic cell.
It will block all electrical transmission along the axon.
explanation: Inhibiting reuptake keeps the neurotransmitter in the cleft longer, increasing its interaction with post-synaptic receptors.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: In epilepsy, what is meant by the term "focus"?
options: The patient's ability to concentrate during a seizure
The specific area of abnormal brain tissue where the hyper-synchronous electrical discharge begins
The lens adjustment on a diagnostic microscope
The target organ of an antiepileptic drug
explanation: Focal seizures originate from a localized area of hyperexcitable neurons, known as the epileptic focus.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: How does "oxidative stress" contribute to neuronal death in neurodegenerative conditions?
options: By increasing the oxygen saturations to 100% in the blood
Through the generation of reactive oxygen species (free radicals) that damage cellular lipids, proteins, and DNA
By preventing the breakdown of acetylcholine
By stabilizing the mitochondrial membrane
explanation: An imbalance between free radical production and antioxidant defenses leads to oxidative damage, a key driver of cell death in diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which neurological structure serves as a physiological barrier that restricts many water-soluble drugs from entering the brain tissue?
options: The basement membrane of the skin
The Blood-Brain Barrier (BBB)
The synaptic cleft
The reticular activating system
explanation: The BBB, formed by tight junctions of endothelial cells and astrocytes, shields the brain from systemic toxins and limits the entry of polar, water-soluble drugs. Presentation 9: Basics of Oxygen Therapy
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: According to the British Thoracic Society (BTS, 2017) guidelines, what is the target oxygen saturation range for most acutely ill patients who are not at risk of hypercapnic respiratory failure?
explanation: For patients without chronic hypercapnic respiratory drive, the target saturation range to prevent hypoxemia while avoiding hyperoxemic toxicity is 94–98%.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: For patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) or a known history of hypercapnic respiratory drive, what is the recommended target oxygen saturation range?
options: 100% strictly
explanation: Patients with hypercapnic drive rely on relative hypoxemia to stimulate breathing. Too much oxygen can suppress this drive, causing hypoventilation and respiratory acidosis.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: In clinical nursing practice, supplementary oxygen must be treated and managed as:
options: A non-prescription comfort therapy that can be adjusted at will
A medication that must be prescribed, complete with target saturation goals and delivery devices
An inert gas that carries no risk of adverse effects
A therapy reserved exclusively for intensive care settings
explanation: Because oxygen has potent physiological effects and potential toxicities, it is classified as a medication and requires a prescriber's order.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which of the following is identified as an early clinical sign that a patient may require supplementary oxygen therapy?
options: Flushed, dry skin and bradycardia
Altered breathing pattern, accessory muscle use, cyanosis, and inability to speak in complete sentences
Worsening pitting peripheral edema
A sudden decrease in the GCS score to 3
explanation: Signs of respiratory distress, hypoxemia, and increased work of breathing are key indicators for initiating oxygen therapy.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the fundamental goal of supplementary oxygen administration as described by Jevon (2020)?
options: To completely eliminate the need for cellular metabolism
To provide an inspired oxygen concentration sufficient to fully saturate available hemoglobin, decreasing the work of breathing and myocardial workload
To increase the rate of renal drug clearance
To convert the patient's respiratory drive from chemical to electrical
explanation: Oxygen therapy aims to ensure adequate tissue oxygenation while reducing the cardiovascular and respiratory effort required to maintain it.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When a patient requires high-flow oxygen during an acute emergency resuscitation, what device is used to deliver an inspired oxygen fraction ($FiO_2$) of up to 85% at a flow rate of 15 L/min?
options: Nasal Cannulae
Simple Face Mask (Hudson Mask)
Non-Rebreather Mask (with reservoir bag)
Venturi Mask
explanation: The non-rebreather mask has a reservoir bag and one-way valves that prevent exhaled air from diluting the oxygen, delivering high concentrations in acute crises.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: To administer a precise, fixed concentration of oxygen (e.g., exactly 28% or 35%) to a patient with a hypercapnic respiratory drive, which delivery device should the nurse select?
options: Nasal Cannulae
Non-Rebreather Mask
Venturi Mask
Simple Face Mask
explanation: Venturi masks use the Bernoulli principle to mix a specific flow of oxygen with ambient air, delivering a highly accurate, fixed fraction of inspired oxygen ($FiO_2$) regardless of the patient's breathing pattern.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the maximum recommended oxygen flow rate when administering oxygen via standard Nasal Cannulae to prevent mucosal irritation and drying?
options: 2 L/min
6 L/min
10 L/min
15 L/min
explanation: Nasal cannulae are low-flow devices. Flow rates above 6 L/min are inefficient, uncomfortable, and dry out the nasal passages.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When utilizing a Bag-Valve-Mask (BVM) resuscitator in an emergency, what is the correct oxygen flow rate to attach to the flow meter to ensure the reservoir bag fills adequately?
options: 2 to 4 L/min
5 to 8 L/min
10 to 12 L/min
15 L/min
explanation: To ensure the reservoir bag remains inflated to deliver 100% oxygen during rescue breathing, the flow meter must be set to 15 L/min.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is a key nursing intervention to prevent skin breakdown in a patient who requires continuous nasal cannulae?
options: Applying petroleum jelly inside the nostrils
Checking and padding the skin behind the ears and around the cheeks regularly
Restricting the patient's fluid intake
Removing the oxygen device for 4 hours each shift
explanation: The plastic tubing of nasal cannulae or masks can cause pressure injuries behind the ears and over the cheekbones, requiring regular skin inspection and protection.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A patient with COPD who is receiving too much oxygen has a rising carbon dioxide level (hypercapnia). What clinical sign should alert the nurse to this complication?
options: Hyper-alertness and rapid speech
Drowsiness, confusion, headache, and a flapping hand tremor (asterixis)
Cold, clammy skin and bradycardia
Sudden development of a pleural rub
explanation: Carbon dioxide retention (narcosis) depresses the central nervous system, leading to lethargy, confusion, headache, and tremors.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: What is the role of the nurse regarding oxygen prescription in an acute ward setting?
options: The nurse can administer any level of oxygen without a prescription indefinitely.
The nurse must ensure a valid oxygen prescription is documented, showing target saturations, and monitor the patient to keep them within that range.
The nurse should only check oxygen levels once a day.
The nurse must always maintain oxygen saturation at 100%.
explanation: Except in emergencies, oxygen is a prescribed medication. The nurse's role is to monitor saturations and titrate flow within the prescribed target range.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which parameter must be checked before placing a humidified oxygen system for a patient?
options: The brand of the oxygen cylinder
The sterile water level and that the connections are tight to prevent infection and leakage
The patient's GNI score
The model of the digital health diary
explanation: Humidifiers use sterile water to moisten dry oxygen gas, requiring regular checks to ensure reservoirs are clean and lines are secure.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: A patient has a respiratory rate of 30, is using accessory muscles, and has an oxygen saturation of 85% on room air. How should the nurse prioritize this situation?
options: Check the patient's baseline GCS in four hours.
Administer emergency high-flow oxygen via a non-rebreather mask and call for immediate medical escalation.
Document the findings and wait for the routine ward round.
Instruct the patient to perform deep breathing exercises.
explanation: Severe hypoxemia accompanied by tachypnea is an emergency requiring immediate oxygenation and rapid clinical escalation.
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Why is smoking strictly prohibited in rooms where oxygen therapy is being administered?
options: Oxygen is highly explosive when combined with tobacco leaves.
Oxygen is a powerful accelerant that supports rapid combustion, creating a major fire hazard.
Nicotine blocks the absorption of oxygen in the lungs.
Tobacco smoke lowers the volume of distribution of oxygen.
explanation: While oxygen itself does not burn, it is an oxidizer that causes materials to ignite much more easily and burn far more intensely. Presentation 10: Assessment and Management of Pain
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: According to Margo McCaffery’s (1983) landmark nursing definition, "Pain" is defined as:
options: An objective physiological reaction to tissue damage
Whatever the experiencing person says it is, existing whenever they say it does
A score of 5 or more on a standardized scale
A transient emotional response to surgical trauma
explanation: McCaffery's definition is a cornerstone of nursing care, emphasizing the subjective nature of pain and the necessity of believing the patient's self-report.pptx].
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: The International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP, 1986) defines "Pain" as:
options: A purely sensory perception that can be measured objectively via vital signs
An unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with actual or potential tissue damage
The activation of nociceptors in the peripheral nervous system
A psychological deficit that resolves with environmental reorientation
explanation: The IASP definition highlights that pain is multidimensional, involving both a sensory component (nociception) and an emotional/affective response.pptx].
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Which classical neurological theory, proposed by Melzack and Wall (1965), explains how cognitive and sensory inputs can modulate the transmission of pain signals in the spinal cord?
options: Orem's Self-Care Agency Theory
The Gate Control Theory
The First-Pass Metabolism Theory
The ABCDE Resuscitation Framework
explanation: The Gate Control Theory suggests that a neural "gate" in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord can open or close to regulate pain signals, influenced by competing sensory inputs (like rubbing) or cognitive factors.pptx].
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: How does "acute pain" differ from "chronic pain" in terms of onset, duration, and physiological purpose?
options: Acute pain is chronic pain that has migrated to a different organ.
Acute pain is sudden, short-term, and serves a protective warning purpose; chronic pain lasts beyond the normal healing time ($>3\text{–}6$ months) and has no adaptive purpose.
Acute pain is purely emotional; chronic pain is purely physical.
Acute pain is managed with non-pharmacological therapies only.
explanation: Acute pain is a protective mechanism warning of damage, while chronic pain is persistent, often outlasts its biological utility, and becomes a disease state in itself.pptx].
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When undertaking a clinical pain assessment, what does the nurse understand about the relationship between a patient's vital signs and their pain experience?
options: Vital signs are highly reliable indicators of chronic pain.
Autonomic compensation (tachycardia, hypertension) can occur in acute pain but is often absent in chronic pain, making self-report the gold standard.
If vital signs are normal, the patient cannot be in pain.
Vital signs always decrease when pain severity increases.
explanation: The body adapts to chronic pain, stabilizing vital signs. Relying on heart rate or blood pressure to verify pain is a common clinical error; self-report remains the most accurate measure.pptx].
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Brennan et al. (2007) suggest that the "unreasonable failure to treat pain" should be viewed as:
options: A standard clinical variation in busy wards
Poor medicine, unethical practice, and an abrogation of fundamental human rights
A necessary step to prevent opioid dependence
An acceptable choice when managing patients with cognitive deficits
explanation: Effective pain management is recognized as an ethical obligation and a human right, making inadequate pain control a significant clinical failure.pptx].
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: Approximately how many people in the United Kingdom are estimated to be living with chronic pain, according to epidemiological data (e.g., Fayaz et al., 2016)?
options: Under 500,000
Around 5 million
Approximately 28 million
Nearly 90% of the population over age 80
explanation: Chronic pain is highly prevalent in the UK, affecting roughly 28 million individuals and representing a massive public health challenge.pptx].
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health

text: When assessing pain in a non-verbal patient, which of the following is considered a reliable behavioral indicator of pain?
options: Normal pupil size and calm breathing
Facial grimacing, moaning, guarding the painful area, and restlessness
Patient sleeping peacefully throughout the assessment
A reduction in heart rate to 60 bpm
explanation: When patients cannot self-report, clinicians must observe non-verbal behaviors (such as muscle tension, vocalizations, and facial expressions) to detect distress.pptx].
subject: Women's & Reproductive Health
